title: Henry Bookshelf
summary: An open-sided bookshelf with matching front and back 2x3 face frames sandwiching the shelves — pocket-holed for strength since the open sides give it nothing else to lean on.
description:
This bookshelf is built from two identical face frames — one front, one back — with the shelves sandwiched between them, which is what lets the sides stay completely open rather than boxed in. Because open sides remove the usual side-panel rigidity, the plan leans hard on pocket holes throughout: the back frame is genuinely easier to build first since its legs and trim are all 2x-thickness lumber, while the front frame mixes in thinner 1x material and needs care to keep hole depth settings correct for each thickness.

Matching the shelf trim placement exactly between the front and back frames is what keeps the whole unit square — build the back frame's trim positions first, then copy them precisely onto the front frame rather than measuring each independently. Leave a 1-1/2" gap at the end of each shelf for the end trim pieces, then measure and cut those end trim pieces to your actual 1x12 width, since 1x12 stock varies enough in real width that a plan number won't fit precisely.

**2x3 lumber is the one nonstandard material here** — most stores don't stock it, but ripping a 2x6 in half on a table saw produces the same actual dimension. Substituting 2x4s is a documented fallback, but changes the shelf trim math by an inch on every board except the top and end trim, so decide on your leg material before cutting anything else.

tools.note: Ripping 2x6s into 2x3s if that size isn't available locally.

materials.name: Primer and paint, or stain and poly

cutList.note: Cut to match your shelf's actual width.
Cut to match your shelf's actual width.
Cut slightly smaller than the opening so it doesn't show from the sides.

steps.title: Build the back frame
steps.body: Set your pocket-hole jig to the 1-1/2" setting and drill pocket holes on each end of the shelf trim boards, and on the tops of the legs. Assemble the back frame with 2-1/2" pocket hole screws.

steps.title: Build the front frame
steps.body: Build the front frame the same way, matching shelf trim placements exactly to the back frame so the shelves come out square.

steps.title: Attach the shelves
steps.body: Set the jig to the 3/4" setting and drill pocket holes around all sides of each shelf (2 per end, 4 per front/back side). Attach the shelves to the front and back frames, leaving a 1-1/2" gap at each shelf end for the end trim.

steps.title: Add the end trim and back panel
steps.body: Measure and cut your end trim pieces to match your actual 1x12 shelf width, since stock width varies. Attach through the pocket holes on the underside of the shelves with 1-1/4" pocket hole screws. Attach the back panel.

steps.title: Finish
steps.body: Sand all surfaces and finish with primer and paint, or stain and poly.
materials: Primer and paint, or stain and poly

unresolvedImages.alt: Open-sided bookshelf with matching front and back face frames
